narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 53.4% chance that you would not pass—tipping just over the threshold that leads to rejection for many applicants. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.40 and LSAT score of 26, alongside your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings, family income in the upper-middle bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. The model weighed these factors against patterns from thousands of past students, focusing on what best predicts bar success. The strongest influence pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking of 1, which stands well below the average of around 6 for those who passed, signaling to the model a notable dip in performance relative to peers who succeeded. Counterbalancing this, your undergraduate GPA of 3.40 played a major positive role, as it's higher than the 3.24 average among bar passers, helping pull the prediction toward success. Your upper-middle family income also contributed favorably, aligning with the bracket most common among those who passed and suggesting supportive resources. Compared to typical accepted students—who often have stronger Year 3 rankings, LSAT scores around 37, and similar full-time plans—your profile showed promise in GPA and background but was held back by that lower Year 3 decile and LSAT, making your overall risk appear higher. While your full-time status matches the norm for passers, it wasn't a top driver here. This close call means small improvements could shift future outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the typical 37 range, strengthening your later-year grades to climb those deciles, and gathering letters highlighting your GPA resilience. Reapplying after addressing these could align you more closely with successful profiles, and many students in similar situations have turned predictions around with targeted prep. You're clearly capable, given your solid undergrad record—keep pushing.
